events.title: Button Book Club
events.description: Do you love to read but hate deadlines? Or are you just too busy to attend traditional book club meetings? If so, our Button Book Club is perfect for you! Each month there will be a new book to read and a brand new pin to collect! How It Works Pick Your Book! Each month will feature a new Easy/Picture, Beginning Reader, Middle Grade, and Teen book. Simply pick which level you want to read and check out the book! Share Your Thoughts! Come back anytime during the month and write/draw your favorite part of the book. Get Your Pin! Each book will have it's own special, collectible pin. Participate every month to collect them all! Plus, get a free lanyard with your first pin to show them off! December Books: Easy/Picture: Pop! Goes the Nursery Rhyme by Betsy Bird Beginning Readers: First Flight by SJ King Middle Grade: The Library of Unruly Treasures by Jeanne Birdsall Teen: His Face is the Sun by Michelle Jabes Corpora

events.title: Teen Passive Book Club: Contemporary
events.description: This month's teen passive book club theme is contemporary! Our featured title is We Are Okay by Nina LaCour: After leaving California to go to college in New York, Marin must face the truth about what happened in the final weeks of summer when her friend comes to visit. Grab a copy (or any alternate title) in the teen area & an activity packet!

events.title: Button Book Club
events.description: Button Book Club is a passive book club, where participants read a book inspired by the monthly theme, fill in a brief worksheet (2 questions) and collect button. In February the topic is Entertainment: movies and music. With a curated selection of books on everything from Ru-Paul to Doll Pardon and Doris Day to Lost Cincinnati concert venues, there is something for everyone. Something else spark your interest? We can't wait to hear what it is. Please see staff if you have any questions.

events.title: Monday Morning Book Club
events.description: The Monday Morning Book Club meets from September to June at the Main Library in the New Books Section on the Main Floor at 10:00 a.m. Please contact book club host, Amy Lychwa, for details. Please note that we have a limited number of books available. As such, copies will be made available to attending members after the meeting and are given on a first-come first-served basis for registered members. Any remaining books will be set aside at the Main Library Circulation desk and will be available to check out. The due date for all book club selections is the next month's meeting date. The timely return of books ensures that book clubs can run smoothly and is an important part of the process. By participating in this program, you acknowledge that you accept and agree to the Guelph Public Library Code of Conduct.Alternative formats are available as per the Accessibility per Ontarians Act by contacting Library Communications at 519-824-6220.Accessible programs and events: Sensory kits are available by request at all library programs. Please let us know a few days before a program if you need any accessibility accommodations so we can plan for you. For sign language interpreters we ask for a minimum of two weeks’ notice. When registering for a program with a support person, please include them as an additional guest.

events.title: Friendship Club
events.description: Join us each month to take part in various activities, adapted for adults with diverse abilities. Play games, talk about books, work on art projects, and more! Support workers welcome. This program is for adults and teens with diverse abilities.

events.title: Monday Book Club
events.description: Looking for a morning of book conversation? Join us on the last Monday each month for a variety of fiction, classic, or nonfiction books. Copies of the books will be available each month at the Circulation Desk. AccessibilityNeed sign language interpretation or other accessibility assistance for this event? events.title: Adult Book Discussion: Finding Grace
events.description: Join us for a discussion of Finding Grace by Loretta Rothschild. "Finding Grace by Loretta Rothschild is a debut novel about a woman named Honor whose seemingly perfect life with her husband Tom and daughter Chloe is upended by a shocking event, leading to a story of love, loss, secrets, and redemption that unfolds through a dual narrative. The plot involves a devastating tragedy, a fateful decision by Tom years later, and the intertwining paths of two women as old love clashes with new."How to AttendThis event takes place in person. Questions about visiting the library? Check out our Using the Library FAQs. AccessibilityNeed sign language interpretation or other accessibility assistance for this event? Please call (312) 747-8184 or email access@chipublib.org to request accommodations. Requests must be made at least 14 business days before the event.

events.title: High Intermediate ESL Book Club - The Murderbot Diaries Vol 1
events.description: Our high intermediate ESL Book Club will read The Murderbot Diaries Vol 1 by Martha Wells. This book club is best for high intermediate English learners. Students will read at home each week. Then we will meet as a group once a week to talk about the story, learn new vocabulary, and practice conversation. Book club will be led by Sheila Jurik. Meeting datesJanuary 12, 26 (No class Jan 19)February 2, 23 (No class Feb 9, 16)March 2, 9, 23, 30 (No class Mar 16)April 13, 20, 27 (No class Apr 6)Meeting time: 10:15am - 11:45amWhere: In the library - your book club leader will tell you which tables. Registration is open now! Book club is limited to 10 participants. Books will be provided for registered participants to keep. Please consider your schedule and do not register if you will miss more than one meeting. The Murderbot Diaries Vol 1 description:On a distant planet, a team of scientists are conducting surface tests, shadowed by their Company-supplied ‘droid ― a self-aware SecUnit that has hacked its own governor module, and refers to itself (though never out loud) as “Murderbot.” Scornful of humans, all it really wants is to be left alone long enough to figure out who it is (and to watch its favorite show in its downtime.) But when a neighboring mission goes dark, it’s up to the scientists and Murderbot to get to the truth.

events.title: The No Book Club
events.description: Join us for an informal meeting to share your thoughts about a book you recently read or a favorite you'd like to recommend. Become acquainted with other readers - in Chicago and elsewhere - and find out what they are reading. You'll probably hear about books you'd like to read and in genres you may not have considered. We'll meet the last Monday of each month except May, November, and December. This program is in partnership with Edgewater Village Chicago and Friends of the Edgewater Library. How to Attend This event takes place on Zoom. Please register on the Chicago Public Library website or at the Edgewater Branch reference desk at least 24 hours before the event. Only one registration per household is needed. You’ll receive an email with a link to the secure Zoom meeting about 24 hours before the meeting. Automatic transcription is included in all CPL events using Zoom. Accessibility Need sign language interpretation or other accessibility assistance for this event? Please call (312) 747-8184 or email access@chipublib.org to request accommodations. Requests must be made at least 14 business days before the event. Attending CPL Online Events Questions about attending online events? Check out our Events FAQs.

events.title: Teen Book Bingo
events.description: Come out to play a classic with a bookish twist- Book Bingo! Find a book to browse for chances to win prizes! Don't know where to start? No problem! We have plenty of books here!

events.title: Adult Book Discussion: Bless Me, Ultima by Rudolfo Anaya
events.description: Join us for a lively discussion of Bless Me, Ultima by Rudolfo Anaya Rudolfo Anaya's Bless Me, Ultima centers on Antonio Márez y Luna and his mentorship under his curandera (a traditional native healer or shaman) and protector, Ultima. It has become the most widely read and critically acclaimed novel in the New Mexican literature canon since its first publication in 1972.How to AttendThis event is in person at the Beverly Branch Library. AccessibilityNeed sign language interpretation or other accessibility assistance for this event? Please call (312) 747-8184 or email access@chipublib.org to request accommodations. Requests must be made at least 14 business days before the event.

events.title: Books & Banter
events.description: Books & Banter is a monthly book club that usually meets on the last Monday of each month to explore books that celebrate and honor the richness of different cultures, movements and milestones. From the inspiring stories of Black History Month and the trailblazing women we recognize during Woman’s History Month to Disability Pride Month and more. Our club delves into diverse perspectives and voices that have shaped the world we live in. With each book we aim to spark meaningful conversations, reflect on history and the joys and struggles of the human experience. Join us Monday March 30th @ 2 pm in the library study room to discuss the book, The Women, by Kristin Hannah. You can request it on the website at hcpl.net or ask for one at the circulation desk.

events.title: Now Read This Book Club at Los Altos Library
events.description: This month, we will be discussing Cursed Daughters: A Novel by Oyinkan Brathwaite. The Los Altos Library Now Read This book club meets on the last Monday of the month to discuss both fiction and non-fiction books that will help us make sense of today’s world. For accessibility accommodations (ex. ASL), please fill out the online form at sccld.org/accessibility 7+days prior to the event. If you need assistance with the form, speak with a staff member at your local library, call (408) 540-3945, or email us at ask@lib.sccgov.org.

events.title: Lit Happens Book Club
events.description: Lit Happens is a new book club for twenty- and thirty-somethings in the Plaza Midwood/NoDa area who love good stories, good vibes, and good coffee. We read across genres, meet monthly, and never judge anyone for choosing the audiobook. Come for the books, stay for the banter. This month, we will be reading Eleanor Oliphant is Completely Fine by Gail Honeyman. Looking for the book? Stop by the branch, put a copy on hold here, or grab a copy at February's meeting! This program will be provided by Maggie at the Plaza Midwood Library. This book club will plan to meet on the last Monday of every month at the Qahwah House in Plaza Midwood at 1318 Pecan Ave in Charlotte, NC. There may be limited parking at the venue, participants are welcome to park at the Plaza Midwood library and walk. Accessibility Statement For in-person programs: Charlotte Mecklenburg Library is committed to providing accessible programs and events. This program will be offered in person at a library location. To request an accommodation, please contact us by phone or email at least one week prior to the program. Requests can be submitted by calling (704) 416-6200 or emailing malbert@cmlibrary.org.

events.title: Readability Book Club
events.description: The Readability Book Club is a group for adults with disabilities that is run in partnership with Center for Independent Futures. We meet weekly on Mondays via Zoom. We read a new book (or books!) every 3 months. Our sessions run from January-March, April-June, July-September and October-December. Each meeting, we will recap what we have read so far, then the facilitator will read the next part of the book aloud. Afterwards, we will discuss what we just read. Our book for January-March will be: WILDCARD by Marie Lu.WildcardRegistration can be done here on the library website or via Center for Independent Futures.

events.title: Adult Book Discussion: Confidence
events.description: Join the Canaryville Branch Adult Book Club for a discussion of the 2023 book, Confidence, by Rafael Frumkin."At seventeen, Ezra Green doesn't have a lot going for him: he's shorter than average, snaggle-toothed, internet-addicted, and halfway to being legally blind. He's also on his way to Last Chance Camp, the final stop before juvie. But Ezra's summer at Last Chance turns life-changing when he meets Orson, brilliant and Adonis-like with a mind for hustling. Together, the two embark upon what promises to be a fruitful career of scam artistry. But when they try to pull off their biggest scam yet--Nulife, a corporation that promises its consumers a lifetime of bliss--things start to spin wildly out of control." - Simon & Schuster"...a propulsive, cheeky, eat-the-rich page-turner to satisfy the craving for a well-crafted caper."- Casey McQuinston for the Washington Post Come into the branch, grab a copy of the book, and then join us for a lively discussion! AccessibilityNeed sign language interpretation or other accessibility assistance for this event? Please call (312) 747-8184 or email access@chipublib.org to request accommodations. Requests must be made at least 14 business days before the event.

events.title: Graphic Novel Discussion: Spent
events.description: Join the Rogers Park Branch as we read and discuss Alison Bechdel's Spent: A Comic Novel.Alison Bechdel, running a pygmy goat sanctuary in Vermont, is existentially irked by a climate-challenged world and a citizenry on the brink of civil war. She wonders: can she pull humanity out of its death spiral by writing a scathingly self-critical memoir about her own greed and privilege? Meanwhile, Alison's first graphic memoir about growing up with her father ... has been adapted into a highly successful TV series. It's a phenomenon that makes Alison ... the envy of her friend group (recognizable as characters, now middle-aged and living communally in Vermont, from Bechdel's beloved comic strip Dykes to Watch Out For). As the TV show ... racks up Emmy after Emmy--and when Alison's Pauline Bunyanesque partner Holly posts an instructional wood-chopping video that goes viral--Alison's own envy spirals. Why couldn't she be the writer for a critically lauded and wildly popular reality TV show--like Queer Eye--showing people how to free themselves from consumer capitalism and live a more ethical life?Books will be available at the Rogers Park Reference Desk by March 1. Accessibility Need sign language interpretation or other accessibility assistance for this event? Please call (312) 747-8184 or email access@chipublib.org to request accommodations. Requests must be made at least 14 business days before the event.

events.title: 星期一下午:西游记 (读书会)
events.description: A book club dedicated to "Journey to the West," the classic Chinese fiction. This club is designed for children who are fans of the story and is conducted entirely in Mandarin Chinese. The event is organized by dedicated teen volunteers from Lynbrook High School.小朋友们，你们听说过《西游记》吗？ 这是中国最有名的神话故事之一，讲述了一段惊险又有趣的旅程。在这个故事里，有聪明勇敢的孙悟空、善良慈悲的唐僧、憨厚搞笑的猪八戒，还有忠诚可靠的沙和尚。他们一路上经历了许多危险和考验，但靠着智慧和团结，成功完成了任务。这次旅程不仅是他们追寻经书的故事，更是一场关于勇气、坚持和成长的冒险！更多有关 美猴王系列丛书. 免费的亲子中文活動. 座位数量有限. 门票将在故事时间开始前 30 分钟在询问处领取。 圖書館中文资源: 大人最新中文书, 中文電子書, 非小說類電子書 (Overdrive) 有歡迎大家一起來借閱電子書. events.description: Welcome to the Tuesday Terrors Horror Book Club! This is a monthly Horror genre book group, run by librarians with a love for all things horror. We meet on the last Tuesday of each month, up on Auahatanga (Level 4) at Tūranga, with the intention of discussing and sharing thoughts relating to horror. Each month we’ll read and discuss books and other formats drawn from a specific subgenre of horror. March – Ecological Horror: Nature’s revenge, climate collapse, bugs, parasitic spores, invasive mold, and fungal infections drive stories of transformation and ecological dread. Come along with anything you’re keen to discuss, horror wise on the theme. We won’t all read the same thing – read whatever you’re most drawn to that fits into the subgenre. We look forward to you joining us. Mike

events.title: Meet the Author of An Island to Myself: Michael N. McGregor
events.description: Seattle-based author Michael N. McGregor will talk about his nonfiction book An Island to Myself: The Place of Solitude in an Active Life. The book, a memoir, is focused on the power of solitude to deepen a life. In his twenties, McGregor traveled to the remote Greek island of Patmos and spent two winter months alone in the depths of winter, 6,000 miles from home. It was a time before cellphones and the internet, when even a phone call was costly. Although he expected his solitude to be meaningful, he wasn't prepared for how it would change him. His book includes later experiences with solitude, too, including a return to Patmos 40 years later. Sponsored by Notre Dame Alumni Club of Seattle Registration not required. Reasonable accommodation for people with disabilities is available by request. Email access@kcls.orgat least seven days before the event. Automated closed captioning is always available for online events.

events.title: Intermediate ESL Book Club - One Foot in the Grape
events.description: Carla's intermediate ESL Book Club will read One Food in the Grap by Carlene O'Neil. This book club is best for intermediate English learners. Students will read at home each week. Then we will meet as a group once a week to talk about the story, learn new vocabulary, and practice conversation. Book club will be led by Carla Passero. Meeting dates: January 13, 20, 27; February 3, 10, 17, 24; March 3, 24, 31. (No class March 10 or March 17).Meeting time: Tuesdays, 7:00pm - 8:00pmWhere: CCC Room next to Member Services DeskRegistration is open now! Book club is limited to 8 participants. Books will be provided at the first meeting for registered participants to keep, If you do not show up at the first meeting and have not made arrangements with the tutor, we will give your spot to the next learner on the wait list. Please consider your schedule and do not register if you will miss more than one meeting. About the book:One Foot in the Grape is a cozy mystery about ex-photojournalist Penny Lively, who inherits a California winery, only to get tangled in murder when her neighbor's wine is sabotaged and a staff member ends up dead in a grape crusher, making her niece the prime suspect, forcing Penny to solve the crime before her family is ruined. It's the first in the Cypress Cove Mystery series, blending wine country charm with amateur sleuthing and danger.

events.title: Books on Tap
events.description: Join us at Books on Tap to discuss Murderland, by Caroline Fraser.What makes a murderer? Pulitzer winner Fraser (Prairie Fires) makes a convincing case for arsenic and lead poisoning as contributing factors in this eyebrow-raising account. Fraser, who was born and raised in the Pacific Northwest, where smelters belted out poison for decades and a proliferation of serial killers in the 1970s and '80s earned the region the nickname "America's Killing Fields," marries a poignant memoir of her Washington State childhood with a vivid catalog of crimes by Ted Bundy, the Green River Killer, and others. The eBook and eAudiobook are available on Libby. This month's location will be The Parkway Tavern MurderlandExplore all of Tacoma Public Library's Book Clubs.

events.description: Join fellow yarn and textile enthusiasts to share inspiration, ideas, and stories from our experiences as knitters, crocheters, and crafters. Indoor programs will be operating at a reduced attendance capacity and will include social distancing based on the library space. Want to be notified about future events? Subscribe to our newsletter here. NOTE: Charlotte Mecklenburg Library is committed to public safety and continues to intentionally and deliberately expand in-person services that align with the COVID-19 safety guidelines set by Mecklenburg County, the Mecklenburg Board of County Commissioners (BOCC), the state of North Carolina and the Centers for Disease Control (CDC). events.title: Mystery Fiction Book Discussion: The Searcher by Tana French
events.description: Book Jacket The SearcherWe welcome all adults who love to read and discuss mysteries. In April, the book will be The Searcher by Tana French. (2020) After years on the Chicago Police force, Cal Hooper retires to a tiny village in western Ireland. His seemingly peaceful retirement comes to an end when a village boy Cal has befriended tells him he's worried about his older brother, Brendan, who has been missing for six months. When Cal asks Brendan's mother about his absence, he discovers that even a tiny community can have dark secrets they want to keep concealed. The Mystery Book Discussion Group meets on the first Wednesday of every other month at 11am in Room 201B. All meetings are sponsored by The Friends of the Cupertino Library. events.title: Men's BYOB (Bring Your Own Book) Book Club
events.description: Join us on the 1st Wednesday of each month at 6pm for the Men's BYOB (Bring Your Own Book) book club! Bring any book you’re currently reading — fiction, nonfiction, audiobook, graphic novel, anything — and join other men for a relaxed, conversation-driven book club. Instead of everyone reading the same title, each participant shares what they’re reading, why they chose it, and whether they’d recommend it. It’s a low-pressure way to discover new books, connect with others, and build a reading community. Men are strongly encouraged to join, but all people are welcome. This program will be provided by North County Regional Library staff. Information about this Group What to Expect: Everyone brings a book they’re currently reading or recently finished Each person shares a 2–3 minute mini-review Open discussion about reading interests, life topics, and book recommendations A casual, supportive space to talk or simply listen Perfect for busy schedules—no assigned reading How to Join an Online Program This event takes place online using Zoom. Please include your email address to receive an email with a link to the secure Zoom meeting at least 24 hours prior to the start of this event. New to using Zoom? Check out these videos to help you get started: Zoom How-To Videos or check out this list for more resources. Accessibility Statement For online programs: Charlotte Mecklenburg Library is committed to providing accessible programs and events. This program will be offered online using the Zoom video conferencing platform. Closed Captioning and Full Transcript will be available during the program. To request an additional accommodation, please contact us by phone or email at least one week prior to the program. Requests can be submitted by calling (704) 416-6000 or emailing ksloan@cmlibrary.org.

events.title: Boos & Brews at Bellbrook Brewing Company:  Knock Knock, Open Wide
events.description: Do you want to explore what the horror genre has to offer? Boos and Brews is for you! Join us at the Bellbrook Brewing Company! for Adults 18+.-- This month we are reading Knock Knock, Open Wide, by Neil Sharpson. Driving home late one night, Etain Larkin finds a corpse on a pitch-black country road deep in the Irish countryside. She takes the corpse to a remote farmhouse. So begins a night of unspeakable horror that will take her to the very brink of sanity. She will never speak of it again. Two decades later, Betty Fitzpatrick, newly arrived at college in Dublin, has already fallen in love with the drama society, and the beautiful but troubled Ashling Mallen. As their relationship blossoms, Ashling goes to great lengths to keep Betty away from her family, especially her alcoholic mother, Etain. As their relationship blossoms, Betty learns her lover's terrifying family history, and Ashling's secret obsession. Ashling has become convinced that the horrors inflicted on her family are connected to a seemingly innocent children's TV show. Everyone in Ireland watched this show in their youth, but Ash soon discovers that no one remembers it quite the same way. And only Ashling seems to remember its star: a small black goat puppet who lives in a box and only comes out if you don't behave. They say he's never come out. Almost never. When the door between the known and unknown opens, it can never close again. Pick up your copy at the Winters Community Library Reference Desk. Boos and Brews meets in the loft at Bellbrook Brewing Company, located at 26 East Franklin Street, Bellbrook, Ohio 45305. A variety of beverages are available for purchase from Bellbrook Brewing Company, To receive updates regarding this event, please register.

events.title: The Short of It: A Short Story Book Club
events.description: A different kind of book club focusing on narratives that can be read in a single sitting. Short stories are a great way to explore genres, authors, and ideas without the commitment of a longer book. Read a unique story every month and meet virtually with other adults for discussion. This month's story is "The Blue's I'm Playing" by Langston Hughes from The Ways of White Folks. First published in Scribner’s Magazine in May, 1934, and in the collection The Ways of White Folks that same year, “The Blues I’m Playing” combines Hughes’s irony, his directness, and his use of dialect. It also conveys powerful messages about race relations, the beauty of blues and jazz, and the black artist’s experiences in the white-dominated world of modern art. The story of a young black pianist, Oceola Jones, and her conflict with her self-appointed white patron, Dora Ellsworth, “The Blues I’m Playing” embodies Hughes’s belief in the fortitude and dignity of black Americans. --Encyclopedia.com How to Attend This event takes place on Zoom. Register at least 24 hours before the event. Only one registration per household is needed. You’ll receive an email with a link to the secure Zoom meeting about 24 hours before the meeting. Automatic transcription is included in all CPL events using Zoom. Accessibility Need sign language interpretation or other accessibility assistance for this event? events.title: Club Book With Nathan Harris
events.description: Literary wunderkind Nathan Harris is the author behind the instant New York Times bestseller The Sweetness of Water (2021), “a historical page-turner about social friction so powerful it ignites a whole town” (NPR). Set in the waning days of the U.S. Civil War and turbulent aftermath of the Emancipation Proclamation, Harris’s debut follows the unlikely partnership and budding friendship between eccentric homesteader George Walker and newly freed Prentiss and Landry. The Sweetness of Water became an Oprah’s Book Club selection and was longlisted for both the Mann Booker Prize and Carnegie Medal for Excellence in Fiction – a rare of honors for any writer, particularly a first-time author still in his twenties. Harris’s anticipated follow-up returns to the Reconstruction era and a South determined to return its Black citizens to bondage. In Amity, Louisiana freedman Coleman treks to Mexico in pursuit of his sister and their former enslaver. In a starred review, Kirkus Reviews commends Amity as “a smartly imagined Western with a different sort of hero… with twists and turns from beginning to end.” Collaborator: MELSA (Metropolitan Library Service Agency). This program is funded with money from Minnesota's Arts and Cultural Heritage Fund.

events.title: Mystery Book Club
events.description: Join the book club to meet, mingle and chat with other literature lovers. Experience the pleasure of reading and discovering new mystery books and authors. For April, the Mystery Book Club is reading The Cartographers by Peng Shepherd."A highly imaginative thriller about a young woman who discovers that a strange map in her deceased father's belongings holds an incredible, deadly secret, one that will lead her on an extraordinary adventure and to the truth about her family's dark history. What is the purpose of a map? Nell Young's whole life and greatest passion is cartography. Her father, Dr. Daniel Young, is a legend in the field and Nell's personal hero. But she hasn't seen or spoken to him ever since he cruelly fired her and destroyed her reputation after an argument over an old, cheap gas station highway map. But when Dr. Young is found dead in his office at the New York Public Library, with the very same seemingly worthless map hidden in his desk, Nell can't resist investigating. To her surprise, she soon discovers that the map is incredibly valuable and exceedingly rare. In fact, she may now have the only copy left in existence because a mysterious collector has been hunting down and destroying every last one along with anyone who gets in the way. But why? To answer that question, Nell embarks on a dangerous journey to reveal a dark family secret and discovers the true power that lies in maps." We meet on the first Wednesday of each month at 6:30 – 7:45 PM in the upstairs Computer Lab. Contact the Library for more information at kw@hcpl.net

events.title: Melinda Myers Webinar: Growing a Bountiful Harvest
events.description: Learn some tips and strategies that will help you to maximize your harvest in any available space. Whether you have a dedicated garden, grow in containers or squeeze fruits, vegetables, and herbs into your other garden beds, we will help you grow lots of produce no matter what your limitations. We will discuss plant varieties as well as planting and harvesting techniques to help increase your vegetable gardening success! Register for this FREE webinar at: https://www.melindamyers.com/about-melinda/upcoming-appearances *The webinar is sponsored by: About Melinda MyersMelinda Myers has written more than 20 gardening books, including the Midwest Gardener’s Handbook, 2nd Edition and Small Space Gardening. She hosts The Great Courses “How to Grow Anything” streaming courses and the nationally syndicated Melinda’s Garden Moment radio program. Myers is also a columnist and contributing editor for Birds & Blooms magazine and writes and nationally syndicated weekly garden column. Before her current media work, Melinda hosted “The Plant Doctor” radio show on WTMJ 620 AM for over 20 years as well as 7 seasons of “Great Lakes Gardener” on PBS. She has a master’s degree in horticulture and was a horticulture instructor with tenure. She was also recently inducted into the National Association for Garden Communicators Hall of Fame.Melinda’s website is www.MelindaMyers.com where you can find gardening videos, gardening tips and information, monthly garden checklists, and more.

To participate, click on the REGISTER button on this page, and include your email address. Once you have registered here, you will be sent the meeting ID and password. You do not have to appear via video if you don't want to - you can join using audio and chat only, if that is your preference. At the scheduled meeting time, click on the link you have been sent to join.

events.title: Silent Book Club
events.description: Do you find it difficult to find time to read? Do you hate reading assigned books? Are you anxious in a traditional book club setting? If you answered yes to any of these, then check out Silent Book Club. A new type of book club, where there's no assigned reading and all readers are welcome.BYOBook (print, eBooks, audiobooks, textbooks, comic books, etc.) and settle in for an hour of silent, sustained reading. At the end of the hour, attendees can discuss what they're reading—or not! A fun way to hear about what others are reading and share what you're reading. Best of all, there's dedicated time each month to read something that interests you. Silent Book Club meets the first Wednesday of the month in Creativity Commons on Level 1. Any person needing an accommodation for a disability to access the benefits of the Library’s services, programs, or activities under the Americans with Disabilities Act should contact the Library by telephone at (972) 292-5669, by email at library@friscotexas.gov, or in writing, not less than five (5) business days prior to the program.

events.title: Intermediate ESL Book Club - Chains (The Seeds of America Trilogy)
events.description: Our intermediate ESL Book Club will read Chains (The Seeds of America Trilogy) by Laurie Halse Anderson. This book club is best for intermediate English learners. Students will read at home each week. Then we will meet as a group once a week to talk about the story, learn new vocabulary, and practice conversation. Book club will be led by Roberta Linsky. Meeting dates: February 26; March 5, 12, 19; April 2, 16; May 7. (No class March 26; April 9, 23, 30.)Meeting time: Thursdays from 10:00am - 11:00amWhere: Tables by the wall posters Registration is open now! Book club is limited to 10 participants. Books will be provided for registered participants to keep. Please consider your schedule and do not register if you will miss more than one meeting. Book description:As the Revolutionary War begins, thirteen-year-old Isabel wages her own fight...for freedom. Promised freedom upon the death of their owner, she and her sister, Ruth, in a cruel twist of fate become the property of a malicious New York City couple, the Locktons, who have no sympathy for the American Revolution and even less for Ruth and Isabel. When Isabel meets Curzon, a slave with ties to the Patriots, he encourages her to spy on her owners, who know details of British plans for invasion. She is reluctant at first, but when the unthinkable happens to Ruth, Isabel realizes her loyalty belongs to whoever can provide her with freedom.

events.title: Virtual Author Talk: Valerie Neal
events.description: In On a Mission, Valerie Neal traces the 45‑year history of U.S. women astronauts, drawing on extensive interviews and archival research to illuminate their achievements, challenges, and cultural impact. She highlights how these trailblazers shaped human spaceflight through more than 100 shuttle missions and numerous long‑duration stays on the International Space Station and Mir. Neal’s work offers an insightful and inspiring account of the women who transformed a once male‑dominated field, capturing both their individual stories and their collective influence on the future of space exploration. ---The Santa Clara County Library District is proud to present a free virtual author‑talk series featuring bestselling, award‑winning, and acclaimed writers from around the world, offering live Q&A sessions two to three times a month. Event links are sent about 24 hours in advance (or upon late registration), recordings are available for a limited time, and a full list of upcoming programs can be found here. ---For accessibility accommodations (ex. ASL), please fill out the online form at sccld.org/accessibility 7+ days prior to the event. If you need assistance with the form, speak with a staff member at your local library, call (408) 540-3945, or email us at ask@lib.sccgov.org.

events.title: Bookin' and Cookin': Listen for the Lie by Amy Tintera
events.description: Join us for a discussion of Listen for the Lie by Amy Tintera. Bring food related to the book to share.---What if you thought you murdered your best friend? And if everyone else thought so too? And what if the truth doesn't matter?After Lucy is found wandering the streets, covered in her best friend Savvy’s blood, everyone thinks she is a murderer. Lucy and Savvy were the golden girls of their small Texas town: pretty, smart, and enviable. Lucy married a dream guy with a big ring and an even bigger new home. Savvy was the social butterfly loved by all, and if you believe the rumors, especially popular with the men in town. It’s been years since that horrible night, a night Lucy can’t remember anything about, and she has since moved to LA and started a new life.But now the phenomenally huge hit true crime podcast "Listen for the Lie," and its too-good looking host Ben Owens, have decided to investigate Savvy’s murder for the show’s second season. Lucy is forced to return to the place she vowed never to set foot in again to solve her friend’s murder, even if she is the one that did it.The truth is out there, if we just listen.

From making traditional art to viewing a new film, each gathering is a fun-filled journey that fosters curiosity and appreciation for the world’s rich diversity. Perfect for ages 6-11, Culture Club invites kids to connect, create, and celebrate together—one culture at a time! events.title: Intermediate ESL Book Club - Skylark
events.description: Our intermediate ESL Book Club will read Sarah, Plain and Tall by Patricia MacLachlan. This book club is best for intermediate English learners. Students will read at home each week. Then we will meet as a group once a week to talk about the story, learn new vocabulary, and practice conversation. Book club will be led by Elaine Hilbelink. Meeting dates: February 27; March 6, 13, 20, 27; April 3 Meeting time: Fridays 10am - 11am Where: Literacy Table Registration is open now! Book club is limited to 10 participants. Books will be provided for registered participants to keep. Please consider your schedule and do not register if you will miss more than one meeting. Skylark description:The second book in the series that began with the Newbery Medal–winning Sarah, Plain and Tall by Patricia MacLachlan. Sarah came to the prairie from Maine to marry Papa. But that summer, a drought turned the land dry and brown. Fires swept across the fields and coyotes came to the well in search of water. So Sarah took Anna and Caleb back east, where they would be safe. Papa stayed behind. He would not leave his land. Maine was beautiful, but Anna missed home, and Papa. And as the weeks went by, she began to wonder what would happen if the rains never came. Would she and Caleb and Sarah and Papa ever be a family again?

events.title: Intermediate ESL Book Club - The Game
events.description: Our intermediate ESL Book Club will read The Game by Monica Hughes. This book club is best for intermediate English learners. Students will read at home each week. Then we will meet as a group once a week to talk about the story, learn new vocabulary, and practice conversation. Book club will be led by Rianne Hastie Adams. Meeting dates: February 27; March 6, 20, 27; April 3, 17. (No class March 13 and April 10.)Meeting time: Fridays 11:30am - 12:30pm Where: Literacy Table Registration is open now! Book club is limited to 10 participants. Books will be provided for registered participants to keep. Please consider your schedule and do not register if you will miss more than one meeting. The Game description:It’s the year 2154. Lisse and her friends have been deemed unemployable in the eyes of society. Now they must scavenge the disintegrating city for food and shelter, just to make ends meet. But their dismal existence starts to look up when Lisse and her friends are invited to participate in The Game, a virtual reality experience where they are challenged to survive. But over time, the line between reality and fantasy starts to blur. What started as a simple exercise quickly becomes a test of endurance, trust, and their will to live.

events.title: Mary Ann Key Book Club Discussion: The Serviceberry
events.description: The Mary Ann Key Book Club uses reading as a catalyst for intercultural conversations around understanding and addressing past and current injustices that impact Black, Indigenous, and People of Color. Join your neighbors in a discussion of our 2026 season selection: "The Serviceberry" by Robin Wall Kimmerer. While Indigenous scientist and author Robin Wall Kimmerer harvests serviceberries alongside the birds, she considers the ethic of reciprocity that lies at the heart of the gift economy and how we can learn from Indigenous wisdom and the plant world to reimagine what we value most. Copies of the book can be reserved; eBooks and eAudiobooks are available through www.hclib.org. Sponsor: Friends of the Hennepin County Library.

events.title: Adv. 3D Printing for Youth
events.description: Ready to take your 3D design skills to the next level? Join us at Advanced 3D Printing for Youth! In this club, we’ll dive deeper into 3D modeling techniques, troubleshoot designs, and experiment with more advanced tools. Whether you want to create functional gadgets, detailed miniatures, or creative prints, this is your chance to push your understanding of 3D printing! EPL staff will provide guidance and feedback as you refine your designs! Each participant’s final design will be 3D printed for free and available for pickup at your local EPL branch. Recommended for ages 9-15 with prior experience in TinkerCAD or 3D design. If attendees want to create an object larger than 6cm x 6cm x 6cm, parents must complete the certification and bring a USB to the class so that the Makerspace can support with processing the print! To 3D print at the Makerspace: An adult (or youth aged 12+) must be certified to 3D print, which is a quick online process found on our website!Book the printer by calling 780-944-5342, email makerspace@epl.ca, or chat face to face with Makerspace staff!Slice the design in the Makerspace, in Children's library, in our class, or on your own devices!Bring the file on a USB to the Makerspace to process. Other branches with on-the-floor 3D printing services are: Abbottsfield, Mill Woods, and Capilano!

events.description: Each month, this book club will discuss books in a variety of genres, from fantasy to contemporary fiction, memoirs to graphic novels, and more! This club's main goal is to chat about books, but also provide a safe space for anyone in the LGBTQIAP+ community who deserve to see a version of themselves within the pages of a book. We also welcome allies wanting to expand their bookish horizons.2026 Book Schedule (subject to change): Jan 3 - Lessons in Magic and DisasterFeb 7 - Chain-Gang All-StarsMar 7 - Forest EuphoriaApr 4 - Is This a Cry for Help?May 2 - AtmosphereJun 6 - The Picture of Dorian GrayJul 11 - The EverlastingAug 1 - The UnworthySep 5 - Read a Queer Romance💖Oct 3 - On Earth We’re Briefly GorgeousNov 7 - Bury Our Bones in the Midnight SoilDec 5 - Holiday Special! (Contact Adam for more info.

events.title: Text & Textiles
events.description: Do you like to read or listen to audiobooks? Do you also like to quilt, sew, knit, crochet or make other fabric art? Join us for a new book club that combines reading and fabric art.At home: Read anything you'd like, or listen to any audiobook you'd like.Make something with fabric, thread, or yarn that reminds you of what you've read. At the library:Bring your book and the project you made to the book club.Tell everyone about the book you read and show off your project.Ideas for fabric art:Crochet a granny square or sew a quilt block in the colors from the cover of the book you read.Embroider a motif that reminds you of something you read.Applique a design from the book.Make a pompom with the colors from the cover of the book. Over time, you'll have enough smaller projects created to combine into a larger project that you can enjoy for years to come. This program takes place in The Spark and is for adults. Registration is not required, but space is limited to the first 15 people. Library staff may take photos, videos, or audio recordings at our events. Please tell us if you do not wish to be photographed, video-recorded, or audio-recorded. We will always respect your requests!An ASL (American Sign Language) interpreter or closed captioning can be provided for this program if requested at least 7 business days in advance. For assistance, please contact your library or accessibility@aclibrary.org.

events.title: Spice of the Season
events.description: Do you love to cook and want to try new flavors and recipes? It’s time to get spicy and join the Spice of the Season Club! The kits will include a small sample of the spice, a brochure with the history of the spice, recipes from the JCPL collection, book recommendations and more. Additional recipes will be provided for each seasonal spice in the months between each spice drop. Spice kits will be available on the first Monday of each quarter, with a limited number available. ¿Te encanta cocinar y quisieras probar nuevos sabores y recetas? Participa en nuestro club del Sabor de la Temporada. Cada temporada tendremos una especie disponible para llevar a casa en un kit preparado. Los kits de especie estarán disponible el primer lunes de cada trimestre (enero, abril, julio y octubre.) Los kits incluyen una muestra de la especia, un folleto con información histórica de la especie, recetas, recomendaciones y más. events.title: Black Comics Club
events.description: Black Comics Club Comics aren’t just stories on a page—they’re whole worlds waiting to be explored. Whether you’ve been collecting comics for years or you’re just picking up your first graphic novel, you’ll find a welcoming space to connect, share, and discover the depth and diversity of Black storytelling.

events.title: Bring Your Own Book Club
events.description: Looking to join a book club without the required reading? The Bring Your Own Book Club meets every month to discuss new titles, share current favorites, and discuss all things books. Whether you read 20 books a month or just got back into reading, you are sure to find new additions for your TBR (To Be Read) List. Join us at The Market @ 7th Street for this program. 2 hours of validated parking is provided for their attached parking garage. Check out their website for more information on visiting.

events.title: Hobbology: Reptiles
events.description: Join us for Hobbology – the (mostly) scientific study of hobbies and interests.🐍 During this interactive animal encounter, meet native North Carolina reptiles, including turtles and snakes. Learn tips to help their wild cousins and also how to keep yourself safe when you are out in their habitat. 🦎🌳 Talk to experts from Stevens Creek Nature Center🔍 Find out how to ID reptiles🐢 Get to know North Carolina's wildlifeJoin us and our friends at Parks and Recreation to get the scoop on snakes!What is Hobbology?Hobbology is a monthly meetup akin to a book club with the goal of exploring new hobbies. We meet on the first Monday of the month.We started this group as a way for young adults, working adults, and students to meet new people and try new things. While this program is aimed at new adults (16-30ish), adults of all ages are welcome to join us! ✨Try something new each month🏆 Build your hobby knowledge📍 Meet other students and working adultsWant to be notified about future events? Subscribe to our newsletter hereAccessibility Statement For in-person programs: Charlotte Mecklenburg Library is committed to providing accessible programs and events. This program will be offered in person at a library location. To request an accommodation, please contact us by phone or email at least one week prior to the program. Requests can be submitted by calling (704) 416-5204 or emailing lcarroll@cmlibrary.org.

events.title: True Crime Podcast Discussion Club
events.description: Do you love true crime? Join us for a true crime podcast discussion club! Twice a month, we’ll meet virtually to discuss a few episodes of a true crime podcast, share our favorites, and get recommendations. Over the next few weeks, we'll be listening to Can I Tell You a Secret?, a series from The Guardian about cyberstalker Matthew Hardy and his web of deception. You can listen via the link above or on Apple or Spotify. Here's what to listen to before we meet: March 9: Episode 1 "The Beginning" and 2 "The Widening Circle"March 23: Episode 3 "The Man Upstairs" and 4 "A Reckoning"April 6: Episode 5 "A Mother's Love," 6 "The Winding Clock" and 7 "An Update" This program is for adults 18 and up due to mature themes, violence, and adult language that may be in the podcasts. These podcasts may contain triggering themes and may feature content warnings. Spoiler prevention: If you listen to episodes beyond the week's assigned episodes, please do not reveal details during our discussion to prevent spoilers! This program will be provided by North County and Matthews library staff. How to Join an Online Program This event takes place on Zoom. Please register by 5:00 p.m. the day before the event. You’ll receive an email with a link to the secure Zoom meeting about 24 hours before the meeting.Want to be notified about future events? Subscribe to our newsletter here.New to using Zoom? events.title: 星期一下午:西游记 (读书会)
events.description: A book club dedicated to "Journey to the West," the classic Chinese fiction. This club is designed for children who are fans of the story and is conducted entirely in Mandarin Chinese. The event is organized by dedicated teen volunteers from Lynbrook High School.小朋友们，你们听说过《西游记》吗？ 这是中国最有名的神话故事之一，讲述了一段惊险又有趣的旅程。在这个故事里，有聪明勇敢的孙悟空、善良慈悲的唐僧、憨厚搞笑的猪八戒，还有忠诚可靠的沙和尚。他们一路上经历了许多危险和考验，但靠着智慧和团结，成功完成了任务。这次旅程不仅是他们追寻经书的故事，更是一场关于勇气、坚持和成长的冒险！更多有关 美猴王系列丛书. 免费的亲子中文活動. 座位数量有限. 门票将在故事时间开始前 30 分钟在询问处领取。 圖書館中文资源: 大人最新中文书, 中文電子書, 非小說類電子書 (Overdrive) 有歡迎大家一起來借閱電子書. Overdirve平台: 小朋友的中文電子書, 大朋友的中文電子小說, 大朋友的非小說類的電子書籍. Hoopla平台: 中文有聲書籍 美国残疾人法案(ADA)申请提供协助 5/25/2026

events.title: Point Reyes Book Club: To The Bright Edge of the World
events.description: The Point Reyes Library Book Club will discuss To the Bright Edge of the World by Eowyn Ivey on Monday, April 6 from 7 to 8 pm. This book club meeting is online. Facilitator - Lorelei Sontag Contact: Mattie Ivy Leeds at 415-663-8375 or mattie.ivyleeds@marincounty.gov to register, or for more information. About the Book: To the Bright Edge of the World by Eowyn IveyIn the winter of 1885, decorated war hero Colonel Allen Forrester leads an exploratory expedition up the Wolverine River and into the vast, untamed Alaska Territory. Leaving behind Sophie, his newly pregnant wife, Forrester records his extraordinary experiences in hopes that his journal will reach her if he doesn't return. As they map the territory and gather information on native tribes, whose understanding of the natural world is unlike anything they have ever encountered, Forrester and his team can't escape the sense that some great, mysterious force threatens their lives. Meanwhile, in Vancouver, Sophie chafes under the social restrictions of a pregnant woman on her own, and yearns to travel alongside her husband. She, too, explores nature, through the new art of photography, unaware that the coming winter will test her own courage and faith to the breaking point. ADA Accommodation Request | Solicitud de Adaptación ADA

events.title: Reading Bingo
events.description: Join us in celebrating Día del Niño (Day of the Child) at the Union City Library throughout April. Pick up a "Reading Bingo" card at the reference desk and complete five reading activities across or diagonally on your own or with a reading friend. Show us your completed bingo card at the reference desk by Saturday, May 2, 2026, for a small prize, while supplies last. Día del Niño (Day of the Child) is celebrated in Mexico every year on April 30. It is a holiday that highlights the wonder and joy children bring into our lives. The library has adapted Día del Niño; we celebrate Día del Niño & Libro by promoting reading and children’s literacy. Acompáñanos a celebrar el Día del Niño en la Biblioteca de Union City durante todo el mes de abril. Recoge tu tarjeta de "Reading Bingo" en el mostrador de consulta y completa cinco actividades de lectura tú solo o con un compañero. Enséñanos tu tarjeta terminada y recibirás un premio pequeño mientras duran existencias. El Día del Niño es celebrado en México cada 30 de abril. Es un día festivo que resalta la maravilla y alegría que los niños comparten con nosotros. El Día del Niño ha sido adaptado por la biblioteca, celebraremos el Día del Niño & Libro promoviendo la lectura y alfabetización infantil. Library staff may take photos, videos, or audio recordings at our events. Please tell us if you do not wish to be photographed, video-recorded, or audio-recorded. We will always respect your requests! An ASL (American Sign Language) interpreter or closed captioning can be provided for this program if requested at least seven (7) days in advance. For assistance, please contact your library or accessibility@aclibrary.org.

events.title: Book Banter: Why We Sleep
events.description: Join us for a fun, engaging discussion of Why We Sleep, by Matthew Walker, PhD. --- Neuroscientist and sleep expert Matthew Walker provides a revolutionary exploration of sleep, examining how it affects every aspect of our physical and mental well-being. Charting the most cutting-edge scientific breakthroughs, and marshalling his decades of research and clinical practice, Walker explains how we can harness sleep to improve learning, mood and energy levels, regulate hormones, prevent cancer, Alzheimer's and diabetes, slow the effects of aging, and increase longevity. He also provides actionable steps towards getting a better night's sleep every night.

events.title: The In-Person Book Club - Daytime Edition
events.description: Join us on the 1st Tuesday of each month at 2:00 pm for The In-Person Davidson Branch Book Club! This month we will be discussing Eddie Winston is Looking for Love by Marianne Cronin. From the publisher: "Eddie Winston is 90 years old. He has lived and he has loved, but he has never been kissed. A true gentleman and incurable romantic, Eddie spends his days volunteering at a charity shop, where he sorts through the donations of the living and the dead, preserving letters and tokens of love along the way. And it is here that he meets Bella, a troubled young woman who, at 24 years old, has just lost the love of her life. When Bella learns that Eddie is yet to have his first kiss, she resolves to help Eddie Winston finally find love, sparking an adventure that will take them to unexpected places and, they hope, bring Eddie Winston to the moment he has waited for all his life. A tale of friendship and kindness that reminds us that those we love are never forgotten and it is never too late to try again." This program will be provided by Davidson library staff. Information about this Group This book club reads across all genres. For a list of all 2026 selections, please click here: In-Person Book Club (Davidson) Looking for more technology resources? events.title: Intersecting Interests - Teen Diverse Media Club
events.description: The world is full of different cultures, artwork, and ways of life--and with the library, you can explore them without ever having to travel! Intersecting Interests is a laid-back media club for like-minded individuals who want to explore diverse media. We'll look at current events, featured diverse YA titles, and share media recommendations from art, poetry, film, and more with fun activities to explore while we discuss what we enjoyed.Topics include race, national origin, LGBTQ+ identity, socioeconomic class, physical and mental ability, education, health care, different religions, philosophies, and more in a supportive setting. You don't need to have read the book ahead of time to enjoy this program. Ages 13-18.Featured Titles and Activities for March-May:March: Wearing My Mother's Heart by Sophia Thakur + personal storytelling modeled after adinkra textiles (originating from Ghana, now widespread in West Africa and among African diasporaApril: As Long as the Lemon Trees Grow by Zoulfa Katouh + how to make rosewater and rose syrup, important for culinary, ritual, and cosmetic uses in Syria as well as many other countries across the Middle East, South Asia, and North Africa.May: The City Beautiful and Wrath Becomes Her by Aden Polydoros + mold a golem from clay to explore Jewish folklore*If there's a book or piece of media you'd like to see featured at a meeting, please feel to contact us with your great ideas!

events.title: Book Clubs of EPL: Ridgerunner
events.description: Love reading? Love to talk about books you've read? Come join us for lively discussions on all types of fiction and non-fiction. We'll feature different titles and authors each month. Come and share your impressions, thoughts and interpretations of a book we have all read. This month we will be reading: Ridgerunner by Gil AdamsonIn November, 1917, William Moreland is in mid-flight. After twenty years, the infamous thief, known as the Ridgerunner, has returned. Moving through the Rocky Mountains and across the border to Montana, the drifter, impoverished and aged beyond his years, is also a widower and a father. And he is determined to steal enough money to secure his son's future. Twelve-year-old Jack Boulton was born in the woods to two outlaws, but now finds himself semi-orphaned and left in the care of Sister Beatrice, a formidable nun of the Anglican Order of Saint Mara. In Banff, Alberta, where tourists, new immigrants, and POWs dwell among the locals, she lays claim to the boy and keeps him in cloistered seclusion in her grand old home. The boy longs to return to his family's cabin, deep in the Sawback Range. His father is coming for him. The nun won't let him go. Set against the backdrop of a distant war raging in Europe and a rapidly changing landscape in the West, Gil Adamson presents a literary Western brimming with a cast of unforgettable characters touched with humour and loss, and steeped in the wild of the natural world.

events.title: Mystery Book Club
events.description: Meet with your neighbors and fellow mystery lovers on the first Tuesday night of each month for a lively discussion of classic and current mysteries. Read this month's selection, drop in and join the fun! For April 7 the club will be discussing Drive your Plow Over the Bones of the Dead by Olga Tokarczuk. SynopsisIn a remote Polish village, Janina devotes the dark winter days to studying astrology, translating the poetry of William Blake, and taking care of the summer homes of wealthy Warsaw residents. Her reputation as a crank and a recluse is amplified by her not-so-secret preference for the company of animals over humans. Then a neighbor, Big Foot, turns up dead. Soon other bodies are discovered, in increasingly strange circumstances. As suspicions mount, Janina inserts herself into the investigation, certain that she knows whodunit. If only anyone would pay her mind.Drive your Plow Over the Bones of the Dead Library staff may take photos, videos, or audio recordings at our events. Please tell us if you do not wish to be photographed, video-recorded, or audio-recorded. We will always respect your requests! An ASL (American Sign Language) interpreter or closed captioning can be provided for this program if requested at least seven (7) days in advance. For assistance, please contact your library or accessibility@aclibrary.org.

events.title: One Village, One Book
events.description: Our book club meets monthly at the library to discuss books set in Oakland and the Bay Area or by Bay Area authors. We select our books from a variety of genres and eras so you’re sure to find something you’re interested in. We’ll get to learn more about our city, meet some neighbors and discuss what we’ve read. This year we’re mostly alternating fiction and non-fiction books. Meetings are the first Tuesday of the month (note the change from 2025!) from 6:30-7:30pm. Come to one discussion, a few or all of them! No pre-registration or commitment is required – all we ask is that you’ve read at least some of the book and are ready to discuss it. Here’s our 2026 schedule: (Quoted descriptions are from the library catalog unless otherwise noted.) January 6, 2026: Sourdough by Robin Sloan (fiction)We’re kicking off the year with a quick read about the very Bay Area intersection of tech and food culture. Lois is an exhausted software engineer who is gifted a very special sourdough starter by her favorite bakers when they must flee the country. Pretty soon she’s part of a mysterious underground food scene at the former Alameda Naval Air Station. February 3, 2026: Family Style: Memories of An American From Vietnam by Thien Pham (graphic memoir)The book that got the most votes from our members on our year-end poll! “Told through the lens of meaningful food and meals, this graphic novel chronicles the author’s childhood immigration to America, where food takes on new meaning as he and his family search for belonging, for happiness and for the American dream.” Pham is a graphic novelist, comic artist and educator based in Oakland. March 3, 2026: Deep Care: The Radical Activists Who Provided Abortions, Defied the Law, and Fought to Keep Clinics Open by Angela Hume (non-fiction)“The story of the radical feminist networks who worked outside the law to defend abortion. Deep Care follows generations of activists and clinicians who orbited the Women’s Choice clinic in Oakland from the early 1970s until 2010, as they worked underground and above ground, in small cells and broad coalitions and across political movements with grit, conviction, and allegiances of great trust to do what they believed needed to be done–despite the law, when required.” April 7, 2026: Moonglow by Michael Chabon (fiction)“A man bears witness to his grandfather’s deathbed confessions, which reveal his family’s long-buried history and his involvement in a mail-order novelty company, World War II and the space program.” Most of it isn’t set in Oakland, but it is the only book on this list that specifically mentions Montclair Library! May 5, 2026: Automatic Noodle by Annalee Newitz (science fiction)Our May read is a slim 163 pages, but packed with interesting ideas. This book is super popular as of December 2025, so you might want to get your hold in early. “In this semicozy tale set in a dystopian near future, robots open their own restaurant, build an unusual found family and achieve personal growth. A few years after the war that led to California’s secession from the United States, four robots awake in the flooded San Francisco takeout place where they were contracted to work.” (Kirkus) “Emboldened by newly granted civil rights from the California government, the bots reboot their lives in classic Bay Area form: by opening a noodle shop…In many ways, Newitz’s near-future San Francisco resembles today’s city, with not-so-subtle nods to AI anxieties, queer identity and the modern-day immigrant experience. It is, in the words of KQED’s Alexis Madrigal, ‘The most San Francisco book that has ever been or could ever have been written.’” (Leah Worthington) (Ebook listed separately) June 2, 2026: Moneyball by Michael Lewis (non-fiction)Baseball season is in full swing, which makes this the perfect time to read Michael Lewis’s nuanced exploration of “how Billy Beene, the general manager of the Oakland Athletics, used a new kind of thinking to build a successful and winning baseball team without spending enormous sums of money.” July 7, 2026: Girls Girls Girls by Shoshana von Blanckensee (fiction)In the summer of 1996, best friends (and secret girlfriends), Hannah and Sam drive from “New York, to the fabled queer paradise of San Francisco, free from the harsh gazes of their neighbors and the stifling demands of Hannah’s devout Orthodox Jewish mother. In San Francisco, they will finally be together as a real couple, out in the open, around other queer people.” But soon they’re working at a strip club, meeting new people and trying to figure out who just they are. August 4, 2026: Power Hungry: Women of the Black Panther Party and Freedom Summer and Their Fight to Feed a Movement by Suzanne Cope (non-fiction)Journey back with us to the summer of 1969 with the story of the free breakfast movement, grocery giveaways and other nurturing mutual aid programs started by the Black Panthers. “NYU writing professor Cope rescues two female activists from obscurity in this intriguing look at the role that food played in the civil rights and Black Power movements.” If you read One Crazy Summer with us in 2025, you’ll enjoy learning the backstory to the Panthers’ food program. September 1, 2026: Kasher in the Rye by Moshe Kasher (memoir)Go back to school with this “hilarious memoir about the absurdity of addiction.” “When he was a young boy, Kasher’s mother took him on a vacation to the West Coast. Well it was more like an abduction…They moved to Oakland, California. That’s where the real fun begins, in the war zone of Oakland Public Schools. Those early years read like part Augusten Burroughs, part David Sedaris, with a touch of Jim Carrol….Brutally honest and laugh-out-loud funny, Kasher…finds humor in even the most horrifying situations.” October 6, 2026: 107 Days by Kamala Harris (memoir)Hopefully by October the miles-long hold list for this book will have settled down enough for you to get a copy! “For the first time, and with surprising and revealing insights, Kamala Harris tells the story of one of the wildest and most consequential presidential campaigns in American history.” (NPR) November 3, 2026: The Giveaway: The Clay Blackburn Story by Owen Hill (crime fiction)We have something noir-ish by an Oakland author for those long November nights. “Hill’s crime fiction is whip-smart, stylish, subversive, hilarious and just dripping with East Bay color, culture and landmarks. Sweet and sour like a perfect Negroni, Hill has invented a new kind of East Bay Noir — the fog rolls in late, the golden afternoon glow somehow both luxurious and ominous in the class war that never sleeps.” (Recommendation from Tally Ho! Books via Oaklandside) December 1, 2026: Sign My Name to Freedom by Betty Reid Soskin (memoir)Reid Soskin, famous for being the oldest park ranger in the history of the National Park Service while working at the Rosie the Riveter National Park in Richmond, has been witness to a wide sweep of American history. She grew up in the Bay Area, raised four kids, ran a record store in Berkeley and more. “Betty just turned 104 and her memoir gives a fascinating look at African Americans migration, the Rosie the Riveter story, and beyond through the Civil Rights era.” (recommender) (Ebook and audiobook listed separately) « See our 2025 books here »« See our 2024 books here »« See our 2023 books here »Learn more about Montclair Library and its Friends group, FOML, here: About | Friends of Montclair Library

events.title: Sci-Fi & Fantasy Book Club
events.description: Join in the discussion of A Corner of White by Jaclyn Moriarty. Sign up at the adult reference desk to get a copy. Madeleine Tully lives in Cambridge, England, the World - a city of spires, Isaac Newton and Auntie's Tea Shop. Elliot Baranski lives in Bonfire, the Farms, the Kingdom of Cello - where seasons roam, the Butterfly Child sleeps in a glass jar, and bells warn of attacks from dangerous Colours. They are worlds apart - until a crack opens up between them; a corner of white - the slim seam of a letter. Elliot begins to write to Madeleine, the Girl-in-the-World - a most dangerous thing to do for suspected cracks must be reported and closed.But Elliot's father has disappeared and Madeleine's mother is sick. Can a stranger from another world help to unravel the mysteries in your own? Can Madeleine and Elliot find the missing pieces of themselves before it is too late? A mesmerising story of two worlds; the cracks between them, the science that binds them and the colours that infuse them.For accessibility accommodations (ex. ASL), please fill out the online form at sccld.org/accessibility 7+ days prior to the event. If you need assistance with the form, speak with a staff member at your local library, call (408) 540-3945, or email us at ask@lib.sccgov.org.

events.title: Through The Looking Glass Book Club - Feature Topic: ATLANTIS
events.description: Do you enjoy myths, legends, folklore, fairy tales and the unexplained? Then come join the Through The Looking Glass Book Club, meeting the second Wednesday every other month at 12PM. Share your book discoveries and favorites on selected topics. Choose 1 to 3 titles from the 2026 Feature Topic Book Suggestion List topic suggestion list or another title on the same topic from the library catalog & register for the program! April's book discussion topic will be ATLANTIS! Greek philosopher Plato wrote about Atlantis over 2,300 years ago, and it has fascinated and inspired people ever since. Whether real or imagined, its story about an advanced civilization and the hubris of humanity still influences adventurers and writers today. Choose a title from the ATLANTIS book suggestion list or another from the library catalog and share your discoveries! Looking for upcoming Through The Looking Glass Book Club topics? Check out our upcoming topic selections here and place a hold on a book from the current topic: ATLANTIS. The topics for 2026 are: Cinderella Stories, Atlantis, Gold, Wolves, Mermaids, and The Sun. View, print or download a flyer of the 2026 book club dates and topics here. (COMING SOON) This program will be provided by Independence Regional Library staff. For more information email cbuchanan@cmlibrary.org or call 704-416-4800 opt. 4. Book Display Books for topic selections are made available in a book display next to the customer holds pick up shelves at Independence Regional Library. Not all books from the list are on the display. Many of the titles on the list that are not on the display are on the shelves at Independence Regional Library. Information about this Group Through The Looking Glass Book Club meets every other month to share favorite books on selected topics.How to Join an Online ProgramThis event takes place on Zoom. events.title: Chronicles of Yarnia Discussion Group
events.description: Join fellow yarn and textile enthusiasts to share inspiration, ideas, and stories from our experiences as knitters, crocheters, and crafters. This program is not led by an instructor--it's a social circle for sharing, building understanding and community. Meet up with other knitters or crocheters for self-directed projects in yarn and thread. Bring your own project to work on--no supplies are provided. While you're here, check out our many crafting books with knitting and crocheting projects to try. We meet weekly, every Wednesday afternoon from 1:00pm to 3:00 pm. This program will take place indoors at the North County Regional Library. Indoor programs will be operating at a reduced attendance capacity and will include social distancing based on the library space. Want to be notified about future events? Subscribe to our newsletter here. NOTE: Charlotte Mecklenburg Library is committed to public safety and continues to intentionally and deliberately expand in-person services that align with the COVID-19 safety guidelines set by Mecklenburg County, the Mecklenburg Board of County Commissioners (BOCC), the state of North Carolina and the Centers for Disease Control (CDC). For the safety of staff and other program participants, please stay home if you feel sick, are experiencing symptoms of COVID-19, have a confirmed case of COVID-19, are awaiting COVID-19 test results, or have recently been exposed to someone with COVID-19.​

events.title: Inquisitive Minds: Nonfiction Book Club
events.description: Join us on the 2nd Wednesday of each month at 3pm for Inquisitive Minds: Nonfiction Book Club! We will meet in the Community Room at SouthPark Regional Library. This month (April) we're discussing: Cue the Sun – The Invention of Reality Show TV by Emily Nussbaum "From beloved New Yorker TV critic Emily Nussbaum comes a groundbreaking narrative detailing the fights, egos, drama, and future presidents of reality television. Cue the Sun is a rollicking, deeply reported story about how the early reality TV business metastasized into an industry that now dominates entertainment in the United States. Starting in 1948, Nussbaum pulls back the curtain on the cultural meat grinder that created a generation-defining form of entertainment, examining shows from The Real World to Survivor to The Apprentice. Through extensive interviews, Nussbaum follows the reality TV industry from its inception with shows like Candid Camera to its 90s heyday and 00s aftermath. The book dives into some of the industry's most remarkable stories--for instance, the one where a serial killer on the run once appeared on The Dating Game" This program will be provided by the SouthPark Regional Library staff. Click here to find the book: https://cmlibrary.bibliocommon... Upcoming Reads:May: Abundance by Ezra Klein & Derek ThompsonJune: Cobalt Red: How the Blood of the Congo Powers Our Lives by Siddharth Kara

Volunteers who register but have not completed the Application Form may have their registration cancelled. Volunteers for this program will earn 2-hours of Community Service each of the 6-weeks. You will be asked to... Prepare program materialsTidy up program materials Providing 1-1 or small group support during an activity or craftRole model appropriate behaviour Registration Required. Want to know more about volunteering at the Library? Visit our website! As a Library volunteer, you are expected to... Be on time and reliable – Show up when you’re supposed to and follow through with your responsibilities.Be responsible and dedicated – Do your tasks well and take them seriously.Respect the staff and follow their lead – Listen to your supervisors and do what they ask.Dress and act appropriately – Wear the right clothes and behave in a professional way at all times.Keep things private – Don’t share personal information about patrons, staff, or other volunteers.

events.title: The In-Person Book Club - Evening Edition
events.description: Join us on the 2nd Wednesday of each month at 7:00 pm for The In-Person Davidson Branch Book Club! This month we will be discussing Eddie Winston is Looking for Love by Marianne Cronin. From the publisher: "Eddie Winston is 90 years old. He has lived and he has loved, but he has never been kissed. A true gentleman and incurable romantic, Eddie spends his days volunteering at a charity shop, where he sorts through the donations of the living and the dead, preserving letters and tokens of love along the way. And it is here that he meets Bella, a troubled young woman who, at 24 years old, has just lost the love of her life. When Bella learns that Eddie is yet to have his first kiss, she resolves to help Eddie Winston finally find love, sparking an adventure that will take them to unexpected places and, they hope, bring Eddie Winston to the moment he has waited for all his life. A tale of friendship and kindness that reminds us that those we love are never forgotten and it is never too late to try again." This program will be provided by Davidson library staff. Information about this Group This book club reads across all genres. For a list of all 2026 selections, please click here: In-Person Book Club (Davidson) Looking for more technology resources? events.title: (Virtual) Pearl Avenue Book Club
events.description: The Pearl Avenue Book Club meets virtually, on the 2nd Wednesday of the month, at 5:30 p.m. All are welcome to join in the lively discussions. Currently, the Club is meeting on zoom. If you register, you will be contacted with the link on the day of the program.Wednesday, April 8, 2026, 5:30 p.m. "Speak to Me of Home," by Jeanine Cummins.On her wedding day in San Juan, Puerto Rico, in 1968, Rafaela Acuña y Daubón has mild misgivings, but she marries Peter Brennan Jr. anyway in a blaze of romantic optimism. She has no way of knowing how dramatically her life will change when she uproots her young family to start over in the American Midwest, unleashing a fleet of disappointments. In the 1980s, against the backdrop of her mother’s isolation in St. Louis, Missouri, Rafaela’s daughter, Ruth, wants only to belong. Eager to fit in, Ruth lets go of her language, habits, and childhood memories of Puerto Rico. It’s not until decades later when Ruth’s own daughter, Daisy, returns to San Juan that her mother and grandmother begin to truly reflect on the choices that have come to define their lives. When a hurricane ravages the island in 2023, leaving Daisy critically injured, Rafaela and Ruth return to the city where their story began. As they gather at Daisy’s bedside, we follow them back into the moments that brought them to this point: we watch as they come of age, fall in love, take risks, and contend with all the heartbreaks, triumphs, and reversals of fortune―both good and bad―that make up a meaningful life. As old memories come to light, so do buried secrets, leaving everyone in the family wondering exactly where it is that they belong.Wednesday, May 13, 2026, 5:30 p.m. TBDWednesday, June 10, 2026, 5:30 p.m. TBDWednesday, July 8, 2026, 5:30 p.m. TBDWednesday, August 12, 2026, 5:30 p.m. TBDWednesday, September 9, 2026, 5:30 p.m. TBDWednesday, October 14, 2026, 5:30 p.m. TBDWednesday, November 11, 2026, 5:30 p.m. TBD Wednesday, December 9, 2026, 5:30 p.m. TBDADA Accommodation Requests

events.description: Quiet Book Club offers a tranquil alternative for book lovers who enjoy the idea of a book club, but often scramble to finish the book or don’t want the pressure of conversation. Bring whatever book you’re reading and spend an uninterrupted hour reading in our cozy library chairs. You have the option to socialize and share afterwards, or just keep reading!Drop-in, no registration required.

events.title: Cookbook Book Club
events.description: Do you love food and cooking? Please join us for the start of a new book club! We choose a different monthly cookbook to take home and prepare one or two recipes. Then, on the second Wednesday of the month, we'll meet and share pictures, stories and reviews of cooked dishes. For April, we will be cooking and discussing recipes from The Route 66 Cookbook by Linda Ly and One Pot One Portion by Eleanor Wilkinson.

events.title: Treat Your Shelf Book Club

Whether it's a favorite or a first read, our discussions are all about sharing thoughts, discovering new perspectives, and getting lost in the words that made these books timeless. This book club meets the 2nd Thursday of each month at University City Regional Library. This month's title is The Catcher in the Rye by J.D. Salinger. Looking for the book? events.title: Getting Cozy Book Club
events.description: If you've never read a cozy mystery before or if you have read thousands, join us each month as we read and discuss the first book in a series. We will meet on the 2nd Thursday of the month at 10 am. What is a cozy mystery? The crime-solver is an amateur sleuth, who eavesdrops, gathers clues, and uses their native intelligence and knowledge of their community to solve the crime. Cozy mysteries are generally considered “gentle” books and most have no graphic violence. The setting is often a small town full of colorful characters.Series frequently have a prominent thematic element introduced by the detective's job, pet, or hobby.These books are plot and character driven with an emphasis on solving puzzles rather than suspense. Our chosen book with serve as our theme for the meeting, we will talk about other book series with a similar theme as well as the recipes, crafts, and more included in many cozies.
